The line integral of the function $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ along the boundary of the circle $ |z| = R $ can be evaluated using contour integration. Since $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ is an entire function (analytic everywhere in the complex plane), the integral over any closed contour is equal to the integral over the real line, which can be computed using the residue theorem. However, because $ f(z) $ has no singularities, the integral is simply the integral of $ e^{z^2} $ around the circle. The result of this integral is $ 2\pi i \times 0 = 0 $, since the function $ e^{z^2} $ is analytic and has no poles or essential singularities. This implies that the energy flow is conserved along the boundary of the circle, which the shaman might interpret as a sign of harmony or balance in the ritual.

The average energy density over the entire area of the circle can be found by computing the integral of the real part of $ f(z) = e^{z^2} $ over the area of the circle. In polar coordinates, $ z = r e^{i\theta} $, and $ e^{z^2} = e^{r^2 e^{i2\theta}} = e^{r^2 (\cos 2\theta + i \sin 2\theta)} $. The real part of this is $ e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} $. The average energy density is then the integral of this over the area of the circle, divided by the area of the circle. This integral can be evaluated using polar coordinates and results in $ \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^R e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} r \, dr \, d\theta $. The result simplifies to $ \frac{2}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R r e^{r^2 \cos 2\theta} dr $. This integral is evaluated by substitution and results in $ \frac{2}{\pi R^2} \times \frac{R^2}{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} $.
